text: The replication crisis of the 2010s reached ego depletion directly. Evan Carter and Michael McCullough conducted a meta-analysis in 2013 suggesting the published literature on ego depletion was riddled with publication bias. Then in 2016, a massive pre-registered multi-lab replication study involving 2,141 participants found no significant ego depletion effect. The field convulsed. Critics argued the replication had used a weak depletion manipulation; defenders of the null result countered that the original paradigm was simply underpowered noise. What emerged was not a clean refutation but a more honest accounting: the effect may be real but smaller and more context-dependent than its early champions believed.
question: What did the 2016 pre-registered multi-lab replication study find regarding ego depletion?

options.text: It confirmed ego depletion with greater statistical power than the original studies.
It found no significant ego depletion effect across 2,141 participants.
It found ego depletion only in participants with low baseline glucose.
It proved that publication bias had no impact on the original findings.
